title: Automorphic Forms - Unipotent orbits and discrete spectrum
speaker: Colette Moeglin

proof_stages.label: Jordan–SL2 parametrization of discrete unipotent data
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls the classification of unipotent orbits in GL_N(C) by Jordan block sizes, their equivalent description by finite-dimensional SL_2(C)-representations, and the parity/multiplicity condition that defines a discrete unipotent orbit with finite sign-valued centralizer characters.

proof_stages.label: Classical groups and their dual-group framework
proof_stages.summary: After fixing a local or global field and a classical group defined by a form, the speaker places the preceding unipotent-orbit data in the Langlands dual classical group, under quasi-split or split hypotheses as needed.

proof_stages.label: Known constructions from discrete unipotent data to spectrum
proof_stages.summary: For discrete unipotent orbits, the representation-theoretic input is orbit data together with a character of the centralizer, with local constructions coming from Lusztig’s finite-group method and theta correspondence and global constructions from theta series and residual Eisenstein series.

proof_stages.label: Langlands parameters as Jordan-block combinatorics
proof_stages.summary: Using the GL_N classification by pairs (ρ,a) as the model, the speaker rewrites conjectural classical-group discrete-series packets as finite-centralizer homomorphisms W_F×SL_2(C)→G^∨, equivalently as auto-dual cuspidal Jordan blocks satisfying parity and dimension conditions plus a centralizer character.

proof_stages.label: Intrinsic definition of Jord(π) and packet questions
proof_stages.summary: For a discrete series representation π, Jord(π) is defined by the irreducibility of induced representations St(ρ,a)×π, and two central questions are posed: whether the expected dimension condition holds and whether the fibers of π↦Jord(π) are Langlands packets.

proof_stages.label: Jacquet-module substitute for the sign character
proof_stages.summary: Since a direct definition of ε_π from normalized intertwining operators is not yet available, a product invariant ε̃_π on pairs of Jordan blocks is introduced using Jacquet-module properties, with an alternating behavior in the cuspidal case.

proof_stages.label: Conditional local classification by Jordan data
proof_stages.summary: Under a deep local hypothesis on reducibility points for induced cuspidal representations, the map π↦(Jord(π),ε̃_π,cuspidal support) is injective with controlled image, and Arthur’s conjecture together with the fundamental lemma supplies the expected dimension condition.

proof_stages.label: Endoscopic stability test for packet fibers
proof_stages.summary: To address the packet-fiber question, the speaker describes a special odd-orthogonal endoscopic setting in which one defines the relevant packet maps and proves stability, modulo a remaining character-sheaf computation for non-split or non-connected groups.

examples.name: Symplectic parity criterion via an L-function pole
examples.note: For a symplectic group, the parity condition on a Jordan block (ρ,a) is illustrated by whether the appropriate local L-function attached to ρ has a pole at s=0, making concrete the otherwise abstract parity rule.
examples.engagement_reason: The symplectic case is given only as a sample parity rule, stating that the integer is even when a related L-function has a pole at s=0, without computing or verifying it for a concrete representation.

passes.reason: the symplectic L-function pole parity rule is a standard classical-group parameter condition used illustratively, not to refute a claim
an ordinary illustrative parity condition for symplectic-group Jordan blocks, not used to refute a claim
It is a standard parity condition for symplectic Langlands/Jordan parameters, used descriptively rather than to refute a claim.
It is an illustrative parity condition for symplectic Langlands/Jordan parameters, not a structurally exceptional object or a refutation.
An illustrative parity condition for the usual symplectic classical family, not a sporadic or boundary object and not used to refute a claim.
It is a standard illustrative parity rule for symplectic Langlands/Jordan parameters, not a structurally exceptional case or a refutation.
the symplectic L-function pole condition is used only to illustrate the general parity rule for Jordan blocks in a classical family
It illustrates the ordinary symplectic parity rule for Langlands/Jordan parameters using an L-function pole, not a failure case or a structurally exceptional object.
used as an illustrative parity condition for symplectic-group parameters via an L-function pole, not as a failure case or structural extreme
It is a representative parity rule for Jordan blocks in the symplectic classical-group case, used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.
It is a standard illustrative parity rule for symplectic-group Jordan parameters via an L-function pole, not an adversarial or structurally exceptional object.
It is a standard illustrative parity condition for Jordan blocks in the symplectic classical-group case, not an adversarial or structurally sporadic object.

examples.name: Odd orthogonal special-parameter endoscopic case
examples.note: The odd orthogonal group with a symplectic decomposition of the dual space and two discrete unipotent elements is used as a worked special case showing how the proposed endoscopic stability picture can be tested.
examples.engagement_reason: The odd orthogonal case is developed as a concrete example: the speaker fixes the group and special parameters, decomposes the symplectic space, describes centralizers and packets, and formulates the endoscopic lifting and stability statement for this setup.

passes.reason: the odd-orthogonal endoscopic setup with special parameters is a systematic classical-family example for stability/endoscopy, not a sporadic, extremal, or adversarial case
a restricted but standard classical endoscopic parameter case for odd orthogonal groups, used illustratively rather than adversarially
It is a restricted but standard endoscopic family for odd orthogonal groups, used as an illustrative case for packets and stability, not as a counterexample.
The odd orthogonal endoscopic parameter is a restricted illustrative case within a classical infinite family, not a sporadic, extremal, degenerate, or adversarial example.
A restricted endoscopic example inside the standard odd orthogonal classical family with special parameters, but not a classification exception, degeneration, or counterexample.
It is a restricted worked family of odd-orthogonal endoscopic parameters used to illustrate stability, not a sporadic, extremal, or adversarial example.
the odd-orthogonal endoscopic example restricts to a very special parameter with the Weil action collapsed to ±1 as a simple test case
It is a restricted but still systematic classical endoscopic family for odd orthogonal groups, used illustratively rather than as an obstruction or counterexample.
a special restricted odd-orthogonal endoscopic case used to demonstrate the method, still within a classical family and not an obstruction
It is a restricted but standard odd-orthogonal endoscopic setting used as a tractable example of stability and lifting, not a sporadic, extremal, degenerate, or adversarial object.
It is used as a tractable endoscopic illustration, with the parameter collapsed to the simplest ±1 Weil-action on a symplectic decomposition.
The odd orthogonal endoscopic setup uses a deliberately restricted but ordinary classical-group parameter family to illustrate stability/endoscopy, not a counterexample or classification exception.
